sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 61 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, I've been spending too many actions exploring the control scheme. Let me step back and think about what I know:
1. The left green square is now at (31, 19) after UP and RIGHT.
2. The right green square is at (31, 40).
3. When the left green square is at (31, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square.
4. The right frame is at (28, 46), so I need to move the right green square from (31, 40) to (28, 46).
5. To move the right green square UP, I need to press UP. But when the left green square is at (31, 19), UP controls the left green square (based on earlier observations).
Wait, let me reconsider. Earlier, when the left green square was at (31, 19), I pressed UP and the left green square moved UP to (28, 19). So UP controls the left green square when it's at (31, 19).
But I need to move the right green square UP from row 31 to row 28. How do I do that?
Maybe I need to move the left green square to a different position to enable UP/DOWN control of the right green square. Or maybe UP/DOWN always controls the left green square, and I need to move the right green square using LEFT/RIGHT only.
Actually, looking at the right green square's position (31, 40), it's already at the rightmost position it can reach (it hit the right frame border). So I can't move it RIGHT anymore. I need to move it UP to row 28 and then RIGHT to col 46.
But if UP controls the left green square, how do I move the right green square UP?
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the control scheme is:
- When the left green square is at (31, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square, and UP/DOWN controls the left green square.
- When the left green square is at (28, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square, and UP/DOWN controls the left green square.
So maybe there's no way to move the right green square UP/DOWN. In that case, maybe the right green square needs to be at row 28 when it's moved RIGHT into the right frame.
Actually, looking at the right frame, it's at (28, 46), which is row 28. The right green square is at (31, 40), which is row 31. To get the right green square into the right frame, I need to move it to row 28.
But if I can't move the right green square UP/DOWN, maybe I need to move the left green square to a position where UP/DOWN controls the right green square.
Let me try moving the left green square DOWN to (34, 19) and see if UP/DOWN then controls the right green square.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the control scheme. Maybe the rule is:
- When the left green square is at the same row as the right green square (row 31), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square.
- When the left green square is at a different row, LEFT/RIGHT controls the left green square.
And UP/DOWN always controls the left green square.
In that case, I can never move the right green square UP/DOWN. So maybe the right green square needs to be moved to the right frame using only LEFT/RIGHT, which means it needs to be at row 28 when I move it RIGHT.
But the right green square is at row 31, and I can't move it UP. So maybe I need to move the right green square to a different column first, then move it UP somehow.
Actually, let me just try pressing UP when the left green square is at (31, 19) and see what happens. If the left green square moves UP, then I know UP controls the left green square. If the right green square moves UP, then I know UP controls the right green square.
Wait, I already did this earlier. When the left green square was at (31, 19), I pressed UP and the left green square moved UP to (28, 19). So UP controls the left green square.
Hmm, but maybe the control scheme changes when the left green square is at a different position. Let me try moving the left green square to (34, 19) and then pressing UP to see if the right green square moves.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just try pressing UP when the left green square is at (31, 19) and the right green square is at (31, 40). If the left green square moves UP, then I'll try a different approach.
Wait, I already know that when the left green square is at (31, 19), UP moves the left green square. So pressing UP won't move the right green square.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not to move the right green square into the right frame. Maybe the goal is to move the left green square into the left frame and the right green square into the right frame, but the right green square is already in the correct position (or close to it).
Actually, looking at the right green square's position (31, 40), it's to the left of the right frame (28, 46). The right frame is at row 28, and the right green square is at row 31. So the right green square is 3 rows below the right frame.
Maybe I need to move the right green square UP 1 time (from row 31 to row 28) and RIGHT 2 times (from col 40 to col 46). But I can't move the right green square UP because UP controls the left green square.
Wait, maybe I need to move the left green square to a position where it doesn't interfere with the right green square's movement. For example, if I move the left green square to (34, 13) (inside the left frame), maybe the right green square can be controlled independently.
Let me try moving the left green square to (34, 13) and then pressing UP to see if the right green square moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('N', 'w', 'c', 'p'):
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols))
        if n['pixels'] <= 20 or n['color'] == 'p':
            print(f"  color={n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}, center={center}")

# Left green at (31, 19), right green at (31, 40)
# I need to move right green to (28, 46)
# Let me try: move left green DOWN to (34, 19), then try UP to see if right green moves
# From (31, 19) to (34, 19): DOWN 1
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
color=p, px=126, center=(31, 35)
  color=c, px=16, center=(28, 46)
  color=w, px=9, center=(28, 46)
  color=N, px=8, center=(31, 19)
  color=N, px=8, center=(31, 40)
  color=c, px=1, center=(31, 40)
  color=c, px=16, center=(34, 13)
  color=w, px=9, center=(34, 13)
